title: Find whether graph is Eulerian (path/circuit) undirected.
description: Find whether graph is Eulerian (path/circuit) undirected.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[0,1]}. Step 2: Compute degrees: deg[0]=2, deg[1]=2, deg[2]=2. Step 3: Count odd degrees: odd_count=0. Step 4: Check connectivity: Start DFS from vertex 0, visited={0,1,2}, all vertices visited, graph is connected. Step 5: Since odd_count=0 and connected, output Eulerian circuit.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: Degrees: deg[0]=1, deg[1]=2, deg[2]=2, deg[3]=1. Step 3: Odd degrees: vertices 0 and 3 are odd, odd_count=2. Step 4: Connectivity: DFS from vertex 0 visits {0,1,2,3}, all visited, connected. Step 5: odd_count=2 and connected, output Eulerian path.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from each vertex to check connectivity, then count odd-degree vertices. Use Euler's theorem: 0 odd-degree vertices means Eulerian circuit, 2 means Eulerian path, otherwise none. This explicitly verifies connectivity rather than assuming it.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Determine Eulerian Path/Circuit in Undirected Graph via Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Implement a complete solution to check if an undirected graph has an Eulerian circuit, Eulerian path, or neither using DFS-based connectivity verification and degree counting.
ladders.explanation: This solution determines Eulerian properties by rigorously applying Euler's theorem with explicit connectivity verification. The intuition is that Euler's theorem (0 odd-degree vertices for circuit, 2 for path) assumes a connected graph, but real graphs might have disconnected components. We verify connectivity by running DFS from each vertex and checking if all vertices are visited. The algorithm first counts degrees for all vertices by iterating through edges. Then, it counts vertices with odd degree. If odd count is 0 or 2, it proceeds to connectivity check: for each vertex, perform DFS to mark all reachable vertices; if any vertex remains unvisited after DFS from a starting vertex, the graph is disconnected. If connected and odd count is 0, it's an Eulerian circuit; if 2, it's an Eulerian path; otherwise, neither. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) due to DFS from each vertex in worst case, and space is O(V+E) for adjacency list and visited array. Connection to L1: This integrates the connectivity verification subroutine with degree counting. Common mistakes include assuming connectivity without verification, mishandling self-loops in degree counting, or incorrect odd-degree thresholds. Real code pattern: Build adjacency list, compute degrees, count odd degrees, then for each vertex run DFS to check if all vertices are visited; if connected, return based on odd count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[0,1]}. Step 2: Compute degrees: deg[0]=2, deg[1]=2, deg[2]=2. Step 3: Count odd degrees: odd_count=0. Step 4: Check connectivity: Start DFS from vertex 0, visited={0,1,2}, all vertices visited, graph is connected. Step 5: Since odd_count=0 and connected, output Eulerian circuit.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: Degrees: deg[0]=1, deg[1]=2, deg[2]=2, deg[3]=1. Step 3: Odd degrees: vertices 0 and 3 are odd, odd_count=2. Step 4: Connectivity: DFS from vertex 0 visits {0,1,2,3}, all visited, connected. Step 5: odd_count=2 and connected, output Eulerian path.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Verify Graph Connectivity via DFS from Each Vertex
ladders.desc: Check if an undirected graph is connected by performing DFS from every vertex and ensuring all vertices are visited.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine verifies graph connectivity, which is crucial for Eulerian property checks. The intuition is that a graph is connected if there's a path between every pair of vertices; we test this by starting DFS from each vertex and checking if it visits all vertices. If any DFS covers all vertices, the graph is connected; otherwise, it's disconnected. Step-by-step: For each vertex v in 0 to V-1, initialize a visited array of size V set to False, run DFS from v to mark reachable vertices, then check if all vertices are visited; if yes for any v, return True (connected), else False after all vertices. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) as DFS runs V times, each O(V+E), and space is O(V+E) for adjacency list and visited. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called after degree counting to confirm connectivity before applying Euler's theorem. Common mistakes include not resetting visited array for each DFS, or stopping early after one DFS without checking all starts. Real code pattern: For v in range(V): visited = [False]*V; dfs(v); if all(visited): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}. Step 2: Start with v=0: DFS from 0 visits {0,1,2}, all visited, so return True. Graph is connected.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: v=0: DFS visits {0,1}, visited[2] and [3] are False, not all. v=1: similar. v=2: DFS visits {2,3}, visited[0] and [1] False. v=3: similar. No vertex covers all, so return False. Graph is disconnected.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Count Odd-Degree Vertices and Apply Euler's Theorem
ladders.desc: Compute vertex degrees, count how many have odd degree, and use Euler's theorem to preliminarily determine Eulerian properties.
ladders.explanation: This core logic applies Euler's theorem for undirected graphs: if all vertices have even degree, there might be an Eulerian circuit; if exactly two vertices have odd degree, there might be an Eulerian path; otherwise, neither. The intuition is that Eulerian paths/circuits require specific degree parity to allow traversal without repetition. Steps: Build an array deg of size V initialized to 0. For each edge [u,v], increment deg[u] and deg[v] (handle self-loops by incrementing twice for u if u==v). Then, iterate through deg, count vertices where deg[i] % 2 == 1. If count is 0, output 'possible circuit'; if 2, output 'possible path'; else 'neither'. Time O(V+E), space O(V). Connection to L1: This output is conditional on connectivity verification. Common mistakes: miscounting degrees for self-loops or parallel edges, or not handling the 'possible' nature without connectivity check. Real code pattern: deg = [0]*V; for u,v in edges: deg[u]+=1; deg[v]+=1; odd=0; for d in deg: if d%2==1: odd+=1; if odd==0: return 'possible circuit'; elif odd==2: return 'possible path'; else: return 'neither'.

examples.trace: Step 1: deg=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: deg[0]=1, deg[1]=1. Edge [1,2]: deg[1]=2, deg[2]=1. Edge [2,0]: deg[2]=2, deg[0]=2. Final deg: [2,2,2]. Step 3: odd count: 0 vertices odd. Step 4: odd==0, so possible circuit.

Step 1: deg=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: deg[0]=1, deg[1]=1. Edge [1,2]: deg[1]=2, deg[2]=1. Edge [2,3]: deg[2]=2, deg[3]=1. Final deg: [1,2,2,1]. Step 3: odd count: vertices 0 and 3 are odd, count=2. Step 4: odd==2, so possible path.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Vertex Degrees in an Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Calculate the degree (number of incident edges) for each vertex in an undirected graph given a list of edges.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes vertex degrees, essential for Eulerian analysis. The intuition is that degree indicates how many edges touch a vertex, affecting traversal possibilities. For an undirected graph, degree is the count of edges incident to a vertex, with self-loops counting twice. Algorithm: Initialize an array deg of size V with zeros. Iterate through each edge [u,v] in the edge list. Increment deg[u] by 1 and deg[v] by 1. If u == v (self-loop), increment deg[u] by an extra 1 (so total +2 for that vertex). Time complexity is O(E) as we process each edge once, and space is O(V) for the deg array. Connection to L2: These degrees are used to count odd-degree vertices. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle self-loops correctly, or misindexing vertices if they are not zero-based. Real code pattern: deg = [0]*V; for u,v in edges: deg[u] += 1; deg[v] += 1; if u == v: deg[u] += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: deg=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: deg[0]=1, deg[1]=1. Edge [1,2]: deg[1]=2, deg[2]=1. Final deg: [1,2,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on an Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Perform DFS traversal from a given starting vertex in an undirected graph to visit all connected vertices.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Intuition: It uses a stack (implicitly via recursion) to visit vertices, marking them as visited to avoid cycles. For an undirected graph, we treat edges as bidirectional. Algorithm: Given adjacency list adj and starting vertex s, initialize visited array of size V set to False. Define recursive function dfs(v): mark visited[v] = True, for each neighbor u in adj[v], if not visited[u], call dfs(u). Start with dfs(s). Time O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once, space O(V+E) for adjacency list and O(V) for visited array plus recursion stack. Connection to L3: DFS is used in connectivity checks. Common mistakes: not handling disconnected graphs, infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks, or stack overflow for large V. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*V; def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for u in adj[v]: if not visited[u]: dfs(u); dfs(s).

examples.trace: Step 1: visited=[False,False,False]. Step 2: dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True, neighbors: [1]. Step 3: dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True, neighbors: [0,2]; 0 visited, so dfs(2). Step 4: dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True, neighbors: [1]; visited. Final visited: [True,True,True].

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False]. Step 2: dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True, neighbors: [1]. Step 3: dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True, neighbors: [0]; visited. DFS stops, vertices 2 and 3 unvisited. Final visited: [True,True,False,False].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Represent an Undirected Graph Using Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list representation from a list of edges for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices. For undirected graphs, each edge is represented twice: once in each vertex's list. Intuition: It efficiently stores sparse graphs and allows quick neighbor access. Steps: Initialize an empty dictionary or list of lists adj of size V. For each edge [u,v] in the edge list, add v to adj[u] and add u to adj[v]. If u == v (self-loop), add u to adj[u] twice or once depending on implementation (typically once, as it's the same vertex). Time O(E) to process edges, space O(V+E) to store lists. Connection to L4: This representation is used for DFS traversal. Common mistakes: not initializing adj for all vertices, forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, or handling duplicate edges. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); if u != v: adj[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0], add 0 to adj[1]; adj becomes [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1], add 1 to adj[2]; adj becomes [[1],[0,2],[1]].

approaches.name: Degree Check with Single DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Count odd-degree vertices and perform a single DFS from any non-zero-degree vertex to check connectivity. Apply Euler's theorem: 0 odd-degree with connected graph means circuit, 2 means path. This optimizes by checking connectivity once.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Determine Eulerian Path/Circuit in Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Complete solution using degree check with single DFS to determine if graph has Eulerian circuit, path, or neither.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires determining whether an undirected graph contains an Eulerian circuit (traverses every edge exactly once and returns to start) or Eulerian path (traverses every edge exactly once but ends at different vertex). The solution combines Euler's theorem with connectivity verification. Euler's theorem states: (1) For Eulerian circuit: all vertices have even degree AND graph is connected (considering only vertices with degree > 0). (2) For Eulerian path: exactly 2 vertices have odd degree AND graph is connected (considering only vertices with degree > 0). Otherwise, neither exists. The algorithm first computes degrees of all vertices, counting odd-degree vertices. If count is 0 or 2, it performs a single DFS from any vertex with degree > 0 to check connectivity among vertices with degree > 0. If DFS visits all such vertices, graph is connected in relevant sense. Based on odd count and connectivity, return appropriate result. Time: O(V+E) for degree computation and DFS. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list and visited array. Connection to L1: This integrates degree counting (L1) with connectivity check (L2) and applies Euler's theorem. Common mistakes: Not handling isolated vertices (degree 0) correctly in connectivity check; forgetting to check connectivity when odd count is 0 or 2; misinterpreting Euler's theorem for path vs circuit. Real code pattern: Build adjacency list, compute degrees and odd count, if odd count not 0 or 2 return 'neither', perform DFS from first vertex with degree > 0, mark visited, after DFS check if all vertices with degree > 0 are visited, based on odd count and connectivity return 'circuit' or 'path' or 'neither'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0: [1,4], 1: [0,2,3], 2: [1,3], 3: [2,4,1], 4: [3,0]. Step 2: Compute degrees: deg(0)=2, deg(1)=3, deg(2)=2, deg(3)=3, deg(4)=2. Odd-degree vertices: 1 and 3 → count=2. Step 3: Since count=2, check connectivity. Vertices with degree>0: all 5. Start DFS from vertex 0 (degree>0). DFS: visit 0, neighbors 1 and 4. Visit 1, neighbors 0,2,3 (0 visited). Visit 2, neighbors 1,3 (1 visited). Visit 3, neighbors 2,4,1 (2 visited). Visit 4, neighbors 3,0 (3 visited). All vertices visited. Step 4: odd count=2 and connected → Eulerian path? Wait, for path need exactly 2 odd, but theorem says circuit requires all even. Here degrees: 1 and 3 odd, others even → exactly 2 odd → Eulerian path. But output says circuit? Let's recompute degrees carefully: Edge list: 0-1,1-2,2-3,3-4,4-0,1-3. Degree 0: edges to 1 and 4 → 2 (even). Degree 1: edges to 0,2,3 → 3 (odd). Degree 2: edges to 1,3 → 2 (even). Degree 3: edges to 2,4,1 → 3 (odd). Degree 4: edges to 3,0 → 2 (even). Yes, odd count=2. So should be path. But wait, check if it's actually circuit? Maybe I miscounted edges. Let's list edges: (0,1), (1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,0), (1,3). That's 6 edges. Vertex 1 has edges to 0,2,3 → degree 3. Vertex 3 has edges to 2,4,1 → degree 3. Others degree 2. So odd count=2 → Eulerian path. But example says circuit? I need to correct. Actually, for Eulerian circuit, all degrees must be even. Here not all even, so cannot be circuit. So output should be 'path'. Let me trace a correct example for circuit. Input: V=3, edges: [[0,1],[1,2],[2,0]]. Degrees: 0:2, 1:2, 2:2 → all even, odd count=0. DFS from 0 visits all vertices (all degree>0). Connected → circuit. So for first example, output should be 'path'. I'll adjust trace. Step 1: adjacency as above. Step 2: degrees as above, odd count=2. Step 3: DFS from 0 visits all vertices (0,1,2,3,4). Step 4: odd count=2 and connected → Eulerian path. Output: 'path'.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0: [1,2], 1: [0,2,3], 2: [1,0], 3: [1]. Step 2: Compute degrees: deg(0)=2, deg(1)=3, deg(2)=2, deg(3)=1. Odd-degree vertices: 1 and 3 → count=2. Step 3: Since count=2, check connectivity. Vertices with degree>0: all 4. Start DFS from vertex 0. DFS: visit 0, neighbors 1 and 2. Visit 1, neighbors 0,2,3 (0 visited). Visit 2, neighbors 1,0 (1 visited). Visit 3, neighbors 1 (1 visited). All vertices visited. Step 4: odd count=2 and connected → Eulerian path? Wait, check degrees: 0 even, 1 odd, 2 even, 3 odd → exactly 2 odd, and connected → should be path. But output says neither? Let's verify connectivity: Graph has edges 0-1,1-2,2-0,1-3. This is connected because from 0 can reach 3 via 0-1-3. So yes connected. Then according to theorem, exactly 2 odd → Eulerian path exists. But maybe the graph has Eulerian path? Let's try to find: Start at vertex 3 (odd degree), path: 3-1-0-2-1? That revisits edge 1-0? Actually need to traverse each edge once. Edges: (3,1), (1,0), (0,2), (2,1). That uses all edges: (3,1), (1,0), (0,2), (2,1). Yes, that's a path 3-1-0-2-1, which uses each edge exactly once and ends at 1 (which is odd degree start 3 and end 1 both odd). So it is Eulerian path. So output should be 'path'. But example says neither. I need a correct example for neither. Input: V=3, edges: [[0,1],[0,2]]. Degrees: 0:2, 1:1, 2:1 → odd count=2. DFS from 0 visits 0,1,2? Edges: 0-1 and 0-2, so graph is connected? Yes, from 0 to 1 and 2. So connected, odd count=2 → path. Actually that is path: start at 1, go 1-0-2. So still path. For neither, need odd count not 0 or 2, or disconnected. Example: V=4, edges: [[0,1],[2,3]]. Degrees: 0:1,1:1,2:1,3:1 → odd count=4. DFS from 0 visits only 0 and 1, not 2 and 3 (disconnected). So neither. So for second example, I'll use that. Input: V=4, edges: [[0,1],[2,3]]. Step 1: adjacency: 0:[1],1:[0],2:[3],3:[2]. Step 2: degrees: 0:1,1:1,2:1,3:1 → odd count=4. Step 3: odd count not 0 or 2 → return 'neither'. Output: 'neither'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Odd-Degree Vertices in Graph
ladders.desc: Compute degrees of all vertices and count how many have odd degree.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because Euler's theorem relies on the number of vertices with odd degree. For an undirected graph, degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it. The algorithm iterates through all edges, incrementing degree for both endpoints. Then, it counts vertices where degree % 2 == 1. Time: O(V+E) to initialize degree array of size V and process E edges. Space: O(V) for degree array. Connection to L0: This count determines whether to proceed with connectivity check; if odd count is not 0 or 2, we can immediately return 'neither'. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize degree array to zero; miscounting due to self-loops (not present in problem as undirected simple graph); not handling isolated vertices (degree 0, which is even). Real code pattern: Initialize degree array of size V with zeros. For each edge (u,v) in edges: degree[u]++, degree[v]++. Then iterate through vertices, count where degree[i] % 2 == 1. Return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize degree array: [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Step 4: Process edge [2,3]: degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1. Step 5: Process edge [3,0]: degree[3]=2, degree[0]=2. Final degrees: [2,2,2,2]. Step 6: Count odd degrees: 0%2=0,1%2=0,2%2=0,3%2=0 → count=0.

Step 1: Initialize degree array: [0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process edge [0,2]: degree[0]=2, degree[2]=1. Final degrees: [2,1,1]. Step 4: Count odd degrees: 0%2=0 (even), 1%2=1 (odd), 2%2=1 (odd) → count=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check Connectivity via DFS on Relevant Vertices
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a starting vertex to verify all vertices with degree > 0 are connected.
ladders.explanation: This sub-problem checks whether the graph is connected, considering only vertices that have at least one edge (degree > 0). Isolated vertices (degree 0) are ignored because they don't affect Eulerian properties. The algorithm performs a single DFS from any vertex with degree > 0. DFS traverses the graph using adjacency list, marking visited vertices. After DFS, we check if all vertices with degree > 0 have been visited. If yes, the graph is connected in the relevant sense; otherwise, it's disconnected. Time: O(V+E) for DFS traversal. Space: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack (or O(V+E) if including adjacency list storage). Connection to L0: This connectivity check is combined with odd-degree count to apply Euler's theorem. Common mistakes: Starting DFS from a vertex with degree 0 (which may not exist if all vertices are isolated); not limiting connectivity check to vertices with degree > 0; using BFS instead of DFS (both work). Real code pattern: Build adjacency list from edges. Find first vertex with degree > 0. Initialize visited array false. DFS(start): mark visited, for each neighbor in adjacency[start], if not visited, DFS(neighbor). After DFS, iterate through vertices, if degree[i] > 0 and not visited[i], return false (disconnected). Else return true (connected).

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]. Step 2: Vertices with degree>0: 0,1,2,3. Step 3: Start DFS from vertex 0 (degree>0). visited=[f,f,f,f]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=true, neighbors: [1]. DFS(1): mark visited[1]=true, neighbors: [0,2] (0 visited). DFS(2): mark visited[2]=true, neighbors: [1,3] (1 visited). DFS(3): mark visited[3]=true, neighbors: [2] (2 visited). Step 4: After DFS, visited=[t,t,t,t]. All vertices with degree>0 are visited → connected.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: 0:[1],1:[0],2:[3],3:[2]. Step 2: Vertices with degree>0: 0,1,2,3. Step 3: Start DFS from vertex 0. visited=[f,f,f,f]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=true, neighbors: [1]. DFS(1): mark visited[1]=true, neighbors: [0] (0 visited). Step 4: After DFS, visited=[t,t,f,f]. Vertices 2 and 3 have degree>0 but not visited → disconnected.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Vertex Degrees from Edge List
ladders.desc: Calculate the degree of each vertex given a list of edges in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is foundational for graph analysis. Degree of a vertex in an undirected graph is the number of edges incident to it. Given an edge list, we can compute degrees by iterating through each edge and incrementing the degree count for both endpoints. This requires initializing an array of size V (number of vertices) to zero. For each edge (u,v), we increase degree[u] and degree[v] by 1. Time: O(E) to process each edge. Space: O(V) for the degree array. Connection to L1: This degree array is used to count odd-degree vertices. Common mistakes: Not handling vertices that appear in no edges (degree remains 0); off-by-one errors if vertices are 0-indexed; assuming edges are unique (they are in problem). Real code pattern: degree = [0]*V; for u,v in edges: degree[u] += 1; degree[v] += 1; return degree array.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize degree array: [0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1. Step 4: Process edge [2,0]: degree[2]=2, degree[0]=2. Final degree array: [2,2,2].
Step 1: Initialize degree array: [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1. Step 3: Process edge [2,3]: degree[2]=1, degree[3]=1. Final degree array: [1,1,1,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Build Adjacency List from Edge List
ladders.desc: Convert a list of edges into an adjacency list representation for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is essential for graph traversal algorithms like DFS. An adjacency list represents a graph as an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a vertex, and the list at that index contains all vertices adjacent to it. For an undirected graph, each edge (u,v) adds v to u's list and u to v's list. The algorithm initializes an empty list for each vertex, then iterates through each edge, appending vertices to each other's lists. Time: O(E) to process each edge. Space: O(V+E) to store the adjacency list. Connection to L2: The adjacency list is used in DFS to traverse neighbors. Common mistakes: Not initializing lists for all vertices; adding duplicate edges (should handle if edges might be duplicated, but problem assumes simple graph); using incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u); return adj.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Graph Degree in Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Learn what degree of a vertex means in an undirected graph and how to identify it.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. In an undirected graph, the degree of a vertex is the number of edges connected to it. Each edge contributes to the degree of both its endpoints. For example, if a vertex has edges to three other vertices, its degree is 3. Isolated vertices have degree 0. The degree is a fundamental property used in many graph algorithms. To find the degree from a visual representation, count the lines touching the vertex. From an edge list, count how many times the vertex appears in the edges. Intuition: Degree indicates how 'connected' a vertex is. Connection to L3: This concept is used to compute degrees from an edge list. Common mistakes: Confusing degree with other metrics like indegree/outdegree in directed graphs; forgetting that self-loops count twice (but not in this problem). Real code pattern: For a given vertex v in a graph with edges list, degree = number of edges where v is either first or second element. Example: edges = [[0,1],[1,2]], degree of vertex 1: appears in [0,1] and [1,2] → degree=2.

examples.trace: Step 1: List edges: [0,1], [1,2], [2,3]. Step 2: Check each edge for vertex 1. Edge [0,1]: vertex 1 is present → count=1. Edge [1,2]: vertex 1 is present → count=2. Edge [2,3]: vertex 1 not present. Step 3: Total degree = 2.

Step 1: List edges: [0,1], [0,2], [1,2]. Step 2: Check each edge for vertex 0. Edge [0,1]: vertex 0 present → count=1. Edge [0,2]: vertex 0 present → count=2. Edge [1,2]: vertex 0 not present. Step 3: Total degree = 2.

approaches.name: Union-Find with Degree Check O(V+E α(V))
approaches.summary: Use Union-Find data structure to check connectivity while counting odd-degree vertices. Build components during edge processing, then apply Euler's theorem based on component analysis. This avoids full graph traversal for connectivity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Eulerian Graph Detection with Union-Find and Degree Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph has an Eulerian circuit, path, or neither using Union-Find connectivity and degree analysis.
ladders.explanation: This solution combines two key insights: Euler's theorem for undirected graphs and efficient connectivity checking via Union-Find. Euler's theorem states that an undirected graph has an Eulerian circuit if all vertices have even degree and the graph is connected. It has an Eulerian path if exactly zero or two vertices have odd degree and the graph is connected (except for isolated vertices). The Union-Find data structure allows us to check connectivity efficiently while processing edges, avoiding a separate BFS/DFS traversal. The algorithm works by initializing a Union-Find structure for all vertices, then processing each edge to update vertex degrees and union the connected vertices. After processing all edges, we count vertices with odd degree and check connectivity of the graph's non-isolated vertices. If all non-isolated vertices belong to the same component and all have even degree, we have an Eulerian circuit. If all non-isolated vertices belong to the same component and exactly two vertices have odd degree, we have an Eulerian path. Otherwise, the graph is not Eulerian. The time complexity is O(V + E α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and space is O(V) for Union-Find arrays and degree counts. This approach is optimal for sparse graphs where we want to avoid O(V+E) traversal after edge processing.

examples.trace:
Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0], degree=[0,0,0,0,0]
Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1, union(0,1): parent=[0,0,2,3,4]
Process edge [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1, union(0,2): parent=[0,0,0,3,4]
Process edge [2,3]: degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1, union(0,3): parent=[0,0,0,0,4]
Process edge [3,4]: degree[3]=2, degree[4]=1, union(0,4): parent=[0,0,0,0,0]
Process edge [4,0]: degree[4]=2, degree[0]=2, union(0,0): no change
Process edge [1,3]: degree[1]=3, degree[3]=3, union(0,0): no change
Final degrees: [2,3,2,3,2]
Odd degree count: vertices 1 and 3 have odd degree (3), count=2
Find roots: all vertices have root 0 (connected)
Result: Connected with exactly 2 odd-degree vertices → Eulerian Path

Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0], degree=[0,0,0,0]
Process edge [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1, union(0,1): parent=[0,0,2,3]
Process edge [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1, union(0,2): parent=[0,0,0,3]
Process edge [2,3]: degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1, union(0,3): parent=[0,0,0,0]
Process edge [3,0]: degree[3]=2, degree[0]=2, union(0,0): no change
Process edge [0,2]: degree[0]=3, degree[2]=3, union(0,0): no change
Final degrees: [3,2,3,2]
Odd degree count: vertices 0 and 2 have odd degree (3), count=2
Find roots: all vertices have root 0 (connected)
Result: Connected with exactly 2 odd-degree vertices → Eulerian Path (Note: Actually Eulerian Circuit requires all even degrees, but this graph has two odd degrees - correction: it should be Eulerian Path, not Circuit)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find Connectivity Check for Non-Isolated Vertices
ladders.desc: Using Union-Find to determine if all non-isolated vertices in a graph belong to the same connected component.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because Euler's theorem requires the graph to be connected (ignoring isolated vertices with degree 0). After processing all edges with Union-Find operations, we need to check if all vertices that have at least one edge (non-isolated) belong to the same component. The algorithm first builds the Union-Find structure by processing all edges with union operations. Then, it identifies a reference vertex that has degree > 0 (non-isolated). For all other vertices with degree > 0, it checks if they have the same root as the reference vertex using the find operation. If any non-isolated vertex has a different root, the graph is disconnected and cannot be Eulerian. The time complexity is O(V α(V)) for checking all vertices, and space is O(V) for the Union-Find structure. This avoids a separate O(V+E) traversal. Common mistakes include not handling isolated vertices properly (they should be ignored in connectivity check) and not using path compression in find operations for efficiency.

examples.trace:
Initialize Union-Find with V=5
Process edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → component {0,1}
Process edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → component {0,1,2}
Process edge [3,4]: union(3,4) → component {3,4}
Non-isolated vertices: 0,1,2,3,4 (all degree>0)
Reference vertex: 0 (degree>0)
Root of 0: find(0)=0
Root of 1: find(1)=0
Root of 2: find(2)=0
Root of 3: find(3)=3
Root of 4: find(4)=3
Vertex 3 has root 3 ≠ 0 → disconnected → false

Initialize Union-Find with V=4
Process edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → component {0,1}
Process edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → component {0,1,2}
Process edge [2,3]: union(2,3) → component {0,1,2,3}
Non-isolated vertices: 0,1,2,3 (all degree>0)
Reference vertex: 0 (degree>0)
Root of 0: find(0)=0
Root of 1: find(1)=0
Root of 2: find(2)=0
Root of 3: find(3)=0
All roots equal → connected → true

ladders.title: Core Logic — Applying Euler's Theorem with Degree Counting
ladders.desc: Determining Eulerian properties based on counts of odd-degree vertices and connectivity.
ladders.explanation: Euler's theorem for undirected graphs provides clear conditions: A graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if all vertices have even degree and the graph is connected (ignoring isolated vertices). It has an Eulerian path if and only if exactly zero or two vertices have odd degree and the graph is connected (ignoring isolated vertices). The core logic involves counting how many vertices have odd degree after processing all edges, then applying these conditions. The count of odd-degree vertices must be 0 for Eulerian circuit, or 0 or 2 for Eulerian path. However, the connectivity condition is equally important—even with correct degree counts, if the graph is disconnected (except for isolated vertices), it cannot be Eulerian. This logic is applied after degree counting and connectivity checking. The intuition comes from graph theory: odd-degree vertices must be endpoints of an Eulerian path (if they exist), and an Eulerian circuit must start and end at the same vertex with no odd degrees. Time complexity is O(V) for counting odd degrees, space is O(1) for counters.

examples.trace:
Count odd degrees: degree[0]=2 (even), degree[1]=2 (even), degree[2]=2 (even), degree[3]=2 (even) → odd_count=0
Check connectivity: connected=true
Apply theorem: odd_count=0 and connected → Eulerian Circuit

Count odd degrees: degree[0]=1 (odd), degree[1]=2 (even), degree[2]=2 (even), degree[3]=1 (odd) → odd_count=2
Check connectivity: connected=true
Apply theorem: odd_count=2 and connected → Eulerian Path

ladders.title: Building Block — Degree Counting During Edge Processing
ladders.desc: Maintaining vertex degree counts while iterating through graph edges.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves calculating the degree of each vertex in an undirected graph by processing the edge list. For each edge [u, v], we increment the degree of both u and v by 1. The degree array is initialized to zeros for all vertices. After processing all edges, degree[i] gives the number of edges incident to vertex i. This is essential for Eulerian graph detection because Euler's theorem depends on vertex degrees. The intuition is simple: each edge contributes to the degree of its two endpoints. Time complexity is O(E) for processing all edges, and space is O(V) for the degree array. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate edges correctly (they should be counted multiple times for degree), or confusing directed/undirected graphs (in undirected, both endpoints get +1).

examples.trace:
Initialize degrees=[0,0,0,0]
Process [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1
Process [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1
Process [2,3]: degree[2]=2, degree[3]=1
Process [3,0]: degree[3]=2, degree[0]=2
Final degrees=[2,2,2,2]

Initialize degrees=[0,0,0]
Process [0,1]: degree[0]=1, degree[1]=1
Process [1,2]: degree[1]=2, degree[2]=1
Final degrees=[1,2,1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Union-Find Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Finding the root representative of an element in a disjoint set with path compression optimization.
ladders.explanation: The find operation in Union-Find returns the root representative of a given element, implementing path compression to flatten the tree structure for future efficiency. The algorithm works recursively or iteratively: starting from element x, follow parent pointers until reaching a node that is its own parent (the root). During this traversal, update each visited node's parent to point directly to the root. This compression reduces future find operations to nearly O(1) amortized time. The intuition is that by making trees flat, we avoid long chains. Pseudocode: function find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x]. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to path compression, space is O(1) extra. Common mistakes include not using recursion properly or forgetting to update parent pointers.

examples.trace:
Start: parent[3]=2 ≠ 3 → find(2)
Find(2): parent[2]=1 ≠ 2 → find(1)
Find(1): parent[1]=0 ≠ 1 → find(0)
Find(0): parent[0]=0 → root=0
Backtrack: set parent[1]=0, parent[2]=0, parent[3]=0
Return root=0, parent becomes [0,0,0,0]

Start: parent[3]=3 → root=3, no compression needed
Return root=3, parent remains [0,1,2,3]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find) Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding how Union-Find represents and merges disjoint sets with parent pointers.
ladders.explanation: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) is a data structure that manages a collection of disjoint sets, supporting two operations: union (merge two sets) and find (determine which set an element belongs to). It represents each set as a tree, where each node has a parent pointer, and the root node represents the set. Initially, each element is in its own set: parent[i] = i. The union operation merges two sets by making the root of one point to the root of the other, often using union by rank (attaching smaller tree under larger) for efficiency. The find operation returns the root of an element's tree. The intuition is like grouping items: we can quickly check if two items are in the same group (find) and merge groups (union). This is foundational for connectivity problems in graphs. Time complexity for m operations on n elements is O(m α(n)) with optimizations, space is O(n).

examples.trace:
Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3]
union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, set parent[1]=0 → parent=[0,0,2,3]
union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=2 → parent=[0,0,2,2]
find(0): parent[0]=0 → root=0
find(3): parent[3]=2, parent[2]=2 → root=2
Sets: {0,1} and {2,3}

Initialize: parent=[0,1,2]
union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, set parent[1]=0 → parent=[0,0,2]
union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, set parent[2]=0 → parent=[0,0,0]
All elements have root 0 → single set {0,1,2}
